channel.title: +00 on रामानुजीये विश्वासटिप्पन्यः
channel.description: Recent content in +00 on रामानुजीये विश्वासटिप्पन्यः

item.title: 1 Contents
item.description:
List of *Images and Map * ix Acknowledgments xi Conventions xiii *The Nāmam: A Note * xv

  1. Introduction: Setting the Stage 1
  2. Traces of Non-Brahmin Śrīvaiṣṇava Leaders 51
  3. Stories from Chennai and Beyond 94
  4. Oral Histories from Puducherry and Beyond 161
  5. Tales from Srirangam, Tanjavur and Beyond 199
  6. Śrīvaiṣṇava Brahmin Voices 227
  7. Temple Politics and the Courts 258
  8. Thinking about the Big Picture 279

Appendix 1: Location of Places Mentioned in Interviews 325 *Appendix 2:*āḻvārs and Earlyācāryas 330 Appendix 3: Changing Definitions of Caste 332 *Appendix 4: The Colonial Cauldron of Anti-Brahmanism * 340

item.title: 2 Images and Map
item.description:
Images

2.1 Non-Brahmin priest at shrine north of

Puducherry with image of the non-Brahmin

scholar Rāmānuja Nāvalar (1829–91). 56 3.1 J. N. B. Chakravarthi Ramanuja Dasar (Chakra)

and his wife Mahalakshmi holding a picture

of Chakra’s grandfather J.J.Thirunarayana

Ramanujar Dasar (1901–83), a Śrīvaiṣṇava

Dalit leader. 97 3.2 Sudarshana ‘Acarya’ leading a send-off

procession for pilgrims going from his ashram

in Minjur (north of Chennai) to Tirupati by foot. 110 3.3 Dr Parandhaman in front of his home shrine in

item.title: 3 Acknowledgements
item.description:
My gratitude goes to the Social Sciences and Research Council of Canada, which supported my fieldwork in India; to McGill University for making me a James McGill Professor with funding that contributed to this project over a decade; and to the Centre for Studies in Religion and Society at the University of Victoria, where I have been an Associate Fellow.

I am especially grateful to all the people whom I inter viewed

item.title: 4 Conventions
item.description: Traditional Śrīvaiṣṇava works are written not only in Sanskrit, Tamil, Maṇ ipravāḷ am and Telugu, but they have also been printed in various scripts (for instance, the Telugu script with its grantha letters has been used for Maṇipravāḷam texts). To facilitate access to Śrīvaiṣṇavism for those who have only a general knowledge about Hinduism, I have favoured Sanskrit spellings for words that are Tamilised Sanskrit (thus, Rāmānuja instead of Rāmānujar or Irāmānujar, or sabhā instead of sabhai, or maṭha instead of maṭam [Tamil]). I give Sanskrit and Tamil equivalents on first use for key terms.
